title: Three-dimensional orbital-free density functional theory description of nuclear pasta in the inner crust of neutron stars
abstract: Background: In the bottom layer of the inner crust of neutron stars, various crystalline structures are expected to emerge that are collectively called ``nuclear pasta.'' It is desired to know properties of nuclear pasta in a wide variety of conditions for astrophysical applications. However, three-dimensional fully-microscopic calculations require huge computational effort that makes it still challenging to carry out systematic calculations.   Purpose: In this paper, we propose an efficient method to calculate various nuclear pasta configurations in a non-empirical manner, based on three-dimensional orbital-free density functional theory (OF-DFT). We demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed approach by applying it to densities throughout the inner crust of neutron stars.   Methods: As a first application of OF-DFT for nuclear pasta, we employ the second-order extended Thomas-Fermi (ETF) expansion of Skyrme energy density functional (EDF) to construct an EDF that depends only on neutron and proton number densities. Based on the variational principle, we derive Euler-Lagrange equations to determine optimal neutron and proton density distributions and solve them self-consistently. In this work, we call this approach the self-consistent ETF (SC-ETF) method.   Results: We perform three-dimensional SC-ETF calculations with various box sizes. We successfully obtain various pasta structures, depending on given average nucleon number densities, consistent with earlier studies. Moreover, we find other exotic structures, such as bending and/or connected rods, slabs with a hole, etc., underlining the advantage of the self-consistent formalism.   Conclusions: We demonstrate that the SC-ETF method proposed in this study, which can be regarded as a realization of OF-DFT, is a promising tool that can efficiently describe complex pasta structures without empirical assumptions on geometric shapes.
summary: This paper introduces an efficient orbital-free density functional theory approach to simulate the complex, non-spherical structures known as nuclear pasta in neutron stars. By avoiding pre-defined geometric shapes, the method successfully discovers exotic new configurations and provides a computationally scalable way to study the neutron star crust.
main_problem: The high computational cost of 3D microscopic calculations (Kohn-Sham DFT) makes it difficult to systematically study the complex, varying geometric structures of 'nuclear pasta' in neutron star inner crusts.
main_result: The authors developed a self-consistent orbital-free density functional theory (SC-ETF) that efficiently identifies both standard and exotic pasta structures (like 'nuclear waffles' or connected rods) without prior geometric assumptions.
method: A self-consistent Extended Thomas-Fermi (SC-ETF) method using a second-order expansion of the Skyrme energy density functional, solved via a 3D gradient descent method on a Cartesian mesh.
model_or_system: The system is the inner crust of a neutron star, consisting of a periodic lattice of protons and neutrons (nuclear pasta) immersed in a background gas of dripped neutrons and electrons.
key_observables: Nucleon number densities (neutron and proton), total energy per nucleon, proton fraction, and geometric configurations of the pasta phases.
important_parameters: Average nucleon number densities, proton fractions, simulation box sizes (up to 40 fm), and mesh spacing (delta x).
main_assumptions: The method uses a second-order (hbar^2) expansion, omitting higher-order shell effects and pairing correlations; electrons are treated as a relativistic homogeneous gas.
figures_summary: Figures show visual examples of exotic pasta shapes (e.g., x-cylinders, slabs with holes), phase diagrams of structures vs. density, and stability analysis of the method as a function of mesh spacing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the computational challenge of nuclear pasta, proposes the SC-ETF/OF-DFT method, demonstrates its ability to find non-empirical shapes, discusses the impact of box size, and concludes with a numerical stability analysis of various Skyrme functionals.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Number densities nq and (b) kinetic densities τq are plotted as a function of a coordinate that passes through the center of 40Ca, calculated with SLy4 ¯h2-ETF EDF and ∆x = 1.2 fm. Neu- tron and proton densities are represented by orange open circles (blue crosses) connected with dashed (solid) lines, while total density is shown by green open triangles connected with dash-dotted lines.

FIG. 2. Contribution of each term in Eq. (37) to the total energy of the system with oscillating density distributions defined by Eq. (36). In panel (a) total energy (without the Coulomb energy) is shown in the A-k plane [where A and k are defined in Eq. (36)], while in the other panels contributions from (b) kinetic term, (c) Cnt term (density dependent term), (d) C∆n t term (n∆n term), (e) Cτt term (nτ term), and (f) C∇J t term (spin-orbit term) are shown. In all the figures, the upper panel and the lower panel show results calculated with SkM∗and SLy4 parameter sets, respectively. We note that the range of the color-bar is fixed to ±5000MeV for all the panels to emphasize the sign of...

FIG. 3. Convergence behavior observed during SC-ETF calcula- tions for µ = 11 MeV with SkM* EDF with a 16fm×16fm×16fm box with a 0.8-fm mesh associated with two different randomly- generated initial densities. The case in which the resulting configu- ration is regarded as a “cylinder” is shown by blue crosses connected with solid lines, while another case in which the resulting config- uration is regarded as a “sphere” is shown by orange open circles connected with dashed lines.

FIG. 4. Nucleon density distributions during the gradient descent iterations for the same calculations shown in Fig. 3. The top row corresponds to the calculation with the cylinder result while the bottom row to that with the sphere result. The minimum and maximum values of color-bars are set to those of nucleon densities of final states shown in (e) and (j) and i shown below each figure is iteration number. Isosurfaces in which the density takes the average value of these maximum and minimum values are also shown. The cylindrical and spherical solutions correspond to ¯n = 0.06630 fm−3 and 0.06624 fm−3, respectively.
